LAW AND MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION, THE COURT ADOPTS THE TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS DENIED AND ITS ALTERNATE MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS DENIED AS TO THE FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION FOR DANGEROUS CONDITION OF PUBLIC PROPERTY AND GRANTED AS TO THE SECOND, THIRD AND FOURTH CAUSES OF ACTION FOR VICARIOUS LIABILITY FOR EMPLOYEE, VICARIOUS LIABILITY FOR INDEPENDENT CONTRACTOR, AND FAILURE TO DISCHARGE MANDATORY DUTY. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jun-14-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Jeremy Cloyd, Esq. 415-988-9828 of Altair Law for Plaintiff Cui Ying Zhou.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative for Summary Adjudication.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the first cause of action for dangerous condition of public property and granted as to the second, third and fourth causes of action for vicarious liability for employee, vicarious liability for independent contractor, and failure to discharge mandatory duty. The City's argument that natural condition immunity bars plaintiffs' claim for dangerous condition of public property lacks merit. There is a disputed issue whether the Canary Island pine tree is a "natural condition." The evidence demonstrates that the City planted the tree in 1957 or 1958 and it is not native to California. (Cloyd Dec., Ex. 3 (the City's response to plaintiff's special rog. 7) and Leggitt Dec. pars. 9-10.) The evidence further shows that the City planted the tree in a non-natural fashion and "up-limbed" it, which contributed to the danger. (Leggitt Dec. pars. 45-46 and 60.) There is also a triable issue whether the property where the injury occurred-a developed urban park-constitutes an "unimproved public property" within the meaning of the statute. (County of San Mateo v. Sup. Crt. (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 724; the City's UMF 4-5; plaintiffs' additional facts 11-13.) The City's argument that trail immunity bars the dangerous condition claim also lacks merit. Government Code 831.4 is inapplicable because there are no allegations that Ms. Zhou was harmed based on the condition, location, or design of the trail. (Toeppe v. City of San Diego (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 921, 931.) In addition, the record is unclear precisely where she was injured and if she was even on the "trail." (City's UMF 1.) The City's objections to the Leggitt declaration are overruled. (Leggitt Dec. pars. 46, 56-57, 60, 62-64, and 77.) There is a triable dispute whether the City created the dangerous condition of public property. (Leggitt Dec. par. 60) A reasonable trier of fact could also conclude that the City had actual or constructive notice of the danger through its prior pruning efforts. (Leggitt Dec., par. 63.) The City is entitled to summary adjudication on the second and third causes of action because plaintiffs do not oppose the motion as to these claims and their remedy is to pursue the dangerous condition of public property claim. The City is entitled to summary adjudication on the fourth cause of action court because plaintiffs do not oppose summary adjudication and the San Francisco Environment Code 1200 et seq. is "advisory," not mandatory. (B.H. v. County of San Bernardino (2015) 62 Cal.4th 168, 180; San Francisco Environment Code 1201(d).)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M.Goodman, Not Reported. =(302/HK)
